lessons.title: What /proc and /sys Are: Virtual Files
lessons.content:
You already know the Linux idea that "everything is a file" — you open, read, and list things on disk with commands like `cat` and `ls`. This lesson adds a surprising twist: a few special folders contain files that aren't really on the disk at all.

**Hook.** Imagine the dashboard of a car. The fuel gauge doesn't store a number anywhere; it shows you a live reading of what's happening right now. `/proc` and `/sys` are folders full of "gauges" like that for your running computer.

**Build.** `/proc` and `/sys` are *virtual* (also called *pseudo*) filesystems. A filesystem is just the tree of folders and files you browse. "Virtual" means there is no real data sitting on the disk underneath. Instead, when you read one of these files, the kernel (the core program that runs the computer and manages its hardware and memory) builds the answer for you on the spot.

So reading a virtual file is really *asking the kernel a live question*. "How much memory is free?" "What kernel version is running?" The kernel computes the answer and hands it back as ordinary text — which means you can use the same plain commands you already know:

```bash
cat /proc/version    # which kernel is running right now
cat /proc/loadavg    # how busy the system is
```

A neat clue that these aren't normal files: `ls -l /proc/meminfo` reports a size of 0 bytes, yet `cat /proc/meminfo` prints lines of text. The size is 0 because nothing is stored; the text only exists for the instant you read it.

**Recap.** `/proc` and `/sys` are windows into the running system. Reading them is cheap and safe — it just asks the kernel a question and shows the answer as text.

items.prompt: What does it mean that /proc is a "virtual" filesystem?
choices: Its files are stored on disk but hidden from normal users
Its files aren't stored on disk; the kernel generates their contents when you read them
It only works inside virtual machines
It is a backup copy of the real filesystem
items.explanation: Virtual (or pseudo) means there is no data on disk. Each read is answered live by the kernel and returned as text. The first choice describes hidden disk files, which is something different.

items.prompt: A /proc file like /proc/cpuinfo isn't a real file on disk. So what is it really?
choices: A snapshot saved at boot time and never updated
A log file the kernel keeps writing to
A live question to the kernel — reading it returns up-to-date information as text
A program you must compile before running
items.explanation: Reading a /proc file asks the kernel for current information, and the answer comes back as text. It is not a saved snapshot or a log being written; the content is produced fresh on each read.

items.prompt: Why does `ls -l /proc/meminfo` show a size of 0 bytes even though `cat /proc/meminfo` prints plenty of text?
choices: The file is empty and cat is making up the output
Nothing is stored on disk, so there is no length to report; the text is built only when you read it
The file is compressed to 0 bytes
You need root permission to see the real size
items.explanation: Because the content is generated on demand and not stored, there is no fixed length, so the size shows as 0. The data appears only at the moment of reading.

items.prompt: Which everyday command can you use to read most files under /proc?
choices: A special tool you must install first
items.explanation: Because the kernel hands back plain text, normal commands such as cat work directly. That is the whole convenience of the everything-is-a-file approach — no special tooling needed.

items.prompt: A good everyday analogy for /proc and /sys is:
choices: A filing cabinet full of old paperwork
A car dashboard whose gauges show the live state of the running system
A photo album of past events
A locked safe you can never open
items.explanation: Like dashboard gauges, these files show a live reading of what's happening right now. A filing cabinet or photo album would imply stored, unchanging data, which is the opposite of these live virtual files.

items.prompt: What is the kernel, in plain terms?
choices: A text editor used to read /proc
The core program that runs the computer and manages its hardware, memory, and processes
Another name for the hard disk
items.explanation: The kernel is the central piece of the operating system. /proc and /sys are how it surfaces its internal state to you as files.

items.prompt: Is it risky to poke around reading files in /proc just to explore?
choices: Yes, reading any /proc file can crash the system
No — reading is cheap and has no side effects, so it's safe to explore
Yes, every read permanently changes a setting
No, but only because /proc is read at boot and then deleted
items.explanation: Reading simply asks the kernel a question; it changes nothing. (Writing to a few special files can change settings, but plain reading is harmless.)

items.prompt: Which command would you use to see which kernel version is currently running?
items.explanation: cat /proc/version reads back the running kernel's version as text. The other commands try to delete, create, or change permissions — none of which is how you ask the kernel a question.

items.prompt: Both /proc and /sys share which key trait?
choices: They store large files that fill up the disk
They are virtual filesystems whose contents come from the kernel, not the disk
They can only be read by automated tools, never by a person
They disappear whenever you log out
items.explanation: Both are virtual filesystems: reads (and a few writes) go to the kernel's in-memory state rather than to disk. The other options describe ordinary disk files or are simply untrue.

lessons.title: Reading the Basics: cpuinfo, meminfo, loadavg
lessons.content:
Now that you know `/proc` files are live questions to the kernel, let's read a few of the most useful ones. You only need `cat`.

**Hook.** Want to know what's inside your machine and how hard it's working — without installing anything? A handful of `/proc` files answer exactly that.

**Build.** Three files cover most quick checks:

- `/proc/cpuinfo` — a description of the processor(s). It prints one block per logical CPU, each starting with a line that begins `processor`. So the number of those lines is the number of CPUs the system sees.
- `/proc/meminfo` — the system's memory accounting. The first lines are the most useful: `MemTotal` (how much RAM exists) and `MemFree` / `MemAvailable` (how much is unused or could be made available). RAM (random-access memory) is the fast working memory programs run in.
- `/proc/loadavg` — the "load average": three numbers showing how busy the system was over the last 1, 5, and 15 minutes. Roughly, it's the average number of tasks waiting to run. Higher means busier.

```bash
cat /proc/cpuinfo            # one block per CPU
grep -c processor /proc/cpuinfo   # count the CPUs
cat /proc/meminfo            # MemTotal, MemFree, MemAvailable...
cat /proc/loadavg            # e.g. 0.12 0.18 0.21 2/431 18922
```

These same numbers are what friendly tools show: `free` (memory) is essentially a tidy reprint of `/proc/meminfo`, and `uptime` (load) reprints `/proc/loadavg`. The tools READ the same files you can read by hand.

**Recap.** To answer "what CPUs do I have, how much memory is free, how busy is the box?" just `cat` `/proc/cpuinfo`, `/proc/meminfo`, and `/proc/loadavg`.

items.prompt: You want to know how much memory is free right now. Which file do you read?
items.explanation: /proc/meminfo holds the memory accounting, including MemFree and MemAvailable. /proc/cpuinfo is about processors and /proc/loadavg is about how busy the system is.

items.prompt: How can you count the number of CPUs the system sees by reading a file?
items.explanation: /proc/cpuinfo prints one block per logical CPU, each starting with a 'processor' line, so counting those lines gives the CPU count. The other options don't relate to CPU count.

items.prompt: The friendly `free` command shows memory usage. Where does it get its numbers?
choices: It measures the RAM chips directly
It guesses based on the CPU speed
It downloads them from the internet
items.explanation: free is essentially a tidy front-end over /proc/meminfo — it reads the same file you can read by hand. It does not talk to the hardware itself.

items.prompt: What does /proc/loadavg tell you?
choices: The amount of disk space left
How busy the system has been over the last 1, 5, and 15 minutes
The MAC address of the network card
The kernel command line from boot
items.explanation: The load average is three numbers describing recent busyness (roughly, how many tasks were waiting to run) over 1, 5, and 15 minutes. The other items live in different files.

items.prompt: In /proc/meminfo, what does MemTotal represent?
choices: The total amount of RAM the system has
The amount of free disk space
items.explanation: MemTotal is the total RAM available to the system. MemFree and MemAvailable describe how much of it is unused; disk space and CPU counts come from other places.

items.prompt: What is RAM, in plain terms?
choices: The permanent storage where files are saved
The fast working memory that running programs use
The network connection to the internet
items.explanation: RAM (random-access memory) is the fast, temporary working memory programs run in. Permanent file storage is the disk; the processor is the CPU.

items.prompt: Which command reads the processor description file?
items.explanation: cat /proc/cpuinfo prints the processor information, one block per logical CPU. The other files describe disks, load, and uptime respectively.

items.prompt: The `uptime` command shows the load average. How does it relate to /proc/loadavg?
choices: It measures load with a separate sensor
It reads the same /proc/loadavg file you could read yourself
It only works if /proc is unmounted
items.explanation: uptime reprints the numbers from /proc/loadavg — the same file you can cat directly. The tool just reads the file and formats it nicely.

items.prompt: Why is reading these /proc files a good first step before installing monitoring tools?
choices: Because the files are encrypted and tools can't read them
Because the same information is right there as text, readable with cat, with nothing to install
Because /proc files are faster than RAM
items.explanation: The kernel already exposes this information as plain text, so cat answers the question immediately. The fancy tools just read the same files.

lessons.title: Per-Process Info: /proc/<pid>/
lessons.content:
So far we've read system-wide files. Now we zoom in on a single running program.

**Hook.** Every running program is called a *process*, and the kernel gives each one an ID number called a PID (process ID). Want to ask one specific process "what are you doing right now?" There's a folder for that.

**Build.** Under `/proc` there is one folder per process, named by its PID — for example `/proc/1492/`. Inside are virtual files that answer questions about just that process:

- `cmdline` — the exact command (with its arguments) that started the process. Its parts are separated by invisible NUL characters, so reading it raw looks run-together; piping through `tr '\0' ' '` adds spaces.
- `status` — a readable summary: the process state, who owns it, and memory use. The field `VmRSS` is the real RAM the process is using right now.
- `fd/` — a folder with one entry per open file the process has. "fd" means file descriptor (the number the kernel uses for each open file or connection). Listing it answers "what files and network connections does this process have open?"
- `maps` — the regions of memory the process is using.

```bash
pid=1492
cat /proc/$pid/cmdline | tr '\0' ' '; echo   # the command that started it
grep VmRSS /proc/$pid/status                  # real RAM in use
ls -l /proc/$pid/fd                           # open files and sockets
```

The everyday tools `ps` and `top` (which list processes) read these very files for you. So when `top` shows a process's memory, it's reporting the same `VmRSS` you can read by hand.

**Recap.** `/proc/<pid>/` is the per-process window: `cmdline` (what started it), `status` (state and memory), and `fd/` (open files). To check a process's open files, list `/proc/<pid>/fd`.

items.prompt: What is a PID?
choices: The ID number the kernel assigns to each running process
The amount of memory a process uses
A type of file on disk
items.explanation: A PID (process ID) is the unique number identifying a running process. /proc has one folder per process, named by its PID.

items.prompt: How would you check which files a process (say PID 1492) currently has open, using only /proc?
items.explanation: Each open file or connection shows up as an entry in /proc/<pid>/fd, so listing that folder answers the question. cmdline shows the start command, and VmRSS shows memory — not open files.

items.prompt: Which /proc/<pid>/ file tells you the exact command that started a process?
items.explanation: cmdline holds the full command line and its arguments. status summarizes state and memory; maps shows memory regions; fd lists open files.

items.prompt: You want the real RAM a process is using right now. Which field do you read?
items.explanation: VmRSS (resident set size) in the per-process status file is the real RAM that process occupies. MemTotal is the whole system's RAM, and load average and fd counts are unrelated to one process's memory.

items.prompt: When you `cat /proc/<pid>/cmdline` the parts look stuck together. Why, and how do you fix it?
choices: The file is corrupted; reboot to fix it
The arguments are separated by invisible NUL characters; pipe through tr '\0' ' ' to add spaces
It needs to be opened in a text editor
items.explanation: cmdline separates arguments with NUL bytes rather than spaces, so they run together on screen. Translating those NULs to spaces with tr makes it readable. It is not corruption or a permission issue.

items.prompt: What does 'fd' stand for in /proc/<pid>/fd?
choices: File descriptor — the number the kernel uses for each open file or connection
items.explanation: fd means file descriptor. Each open file, socket, or pipe gets a numbered descriptor, and they appear as entries in the fd/ folder.

items.prompt: When the `top` command shows a process's memory usage, where does that number ultimately come from?
choices: From the process folder under /proc that top reads
items.explanation: top (like ps) reads the per-process /proc files — such as status/VmRSS — to display memory. The tools surface the same data you can read by hand.

items.prompt: What does /proc/<pid>/status contain?
choices: The full source code of the program
A readable summary including the process state, its owner, and memory use such as VmRSS
A list of every other process on the system
items.explanation: status is a human-readable summary of one process: its state, owner, and memory fields like VmRSS. It does not contain source code or system-wide lists.

items.prompt: If you read /proc/meminfo and then add up the memory of every process via their /proc/<pid>/status, why might the numbers not line up neatly?
choices: Because system memory accounting (shared memory, caches) is more than the sum of per-process resident sets
Because processes hide their real memory
items.explanation: System-wide memory includes shared pages and caches that aren't simply the sum of each process's VmRSS, so reconciling free/meminfo with per-process numbers takes care. The data isn't random or fake.

lessons.title: /sys: The Map of Your Hardware
lessons.content:
`/proc` is mostly about processes and overall system info. Its sibling `/sys` is about *hardware*.

**Hook.** How does the system know what devices you have — network cards, disks, USB gadgets — and their current state, like whether the network cable is plugged in? It keeps a tidy map of them under `/sys`, and you read it with `cat`.

**Build.** `/sys` (the sysfs filesystem) is the kernel's *device model*: a set of folders describing every device the kernel knows about, laid out as files. Like `/proc`, these are virtual files — reading one asks the kernel for that device's live state.

A few useful places:

- `/sys/devices/` — the real, full tree of devices. It's detailed and not the friendliest to browse.
- `/sys/class/<type>/` — devices grouped by what they do. For example `/sys/class/net/` lists network interfaces; `/sys/block/` lists whole disks.
- `/sys/module/` — information about loaded kernel modules (add-on pieces of the kernel, such as a driver for a specific device).

The friendly views like `/sys/class` are mostly *shortcuts* (symbolic links) pointing into the real `/sys/devices/` tree. So one device shows up in several places — a detailed home and easier-to-find groupings.

```bash
ls /sys/class/net                     # lo  eth0  wlan0 ...
cat /sys/class/net/eth0/operstate     # up  or  down
cat /sys/class/net/eth0/address       # the MAC (hardware address)
```

The tool `ip link` shows this same interface state — it READS these files for you.

**Recap.** `/sys` is the live map of your hardware. To check a network interface's state without any special tool, read `/sys/class/net/<name>/operstate`.

items.prompt: What is /sys mainly about?
choices: The kernel's model of hardware devices, exposed as files
Saved log files from past boots
items.explanation: /sys (sysfs) is the device model: folders and files describing the hardware the kernel knows about. Processes live under /proc instead.

items.prompt: Where would you look to see the list of network interfaces?
items.explanation: /sys/class/net/ groups the network interfaces. /sys/module is about kernel modules, and the /proc files listed are about memory and load.

items.prompt: How do you check whether the interface eth0 is currently up or down, with no special tool?
items.explanation: operstate reports up or down for that interface. The address file gives the MAC instead, and the other paths are unrelated to link state.

items.prompt: The /sys/class views are described as shortcuts. What does that mean?
choices: They are copies that can become out of date
They are symbolic links pointing into the real /sys/devices tree
They only appear after you run a command
items.explanation: /sys/class entries are symbolic links into the canonical /sys/devices tree, so one device appears in several convenient groupings. They are not separate copies that drift out of date.

items.prompt: Which directory holds the real, full tree of devices?
items.explanation: /sys/devices/ is the canonical device tree. /sys/class and /sys/block are friendlier grouped views (mostly symlinks) into it.

items.prompt: A kernel module is best described as:
choices: A user account with extra privileges
An add-on piece of the kernel, such as a driver for a specific device, that can be loaded in
items.explanation: A kernel module is an add-on component (often a device driver) that extends the kernel. Information about loaded modules appears under /sys/module/.

items.prompt: The `ip link` command shows network interface state. How does it relate to /sys/class/net?
choices: It ignores /sys and queries a remote server
It reads the same per-interface files under /sys (and related kernel sources) and formats them
It only works if /sys is deleted first
items.explanation: ip link surfaces the same live interface information that lives under /sys/class/net (operstate, address, etc.). The tool reads it for you.

items.prompt: Are files under /sys real files stored on disk?
choices: Yes, they take up disk space like any document
No — like /proc, sysfs is virtual; reading a file asks the kernel for the device's live state
Only the ones under /sys/devices are real
Yes, but only after the first reboot
items.explanation: /sys is a virtual filesystem just like /proc. Reading a file returns the device's current state from the kernel, not data stored on disk.

items.prompt: Which /sys file would show a network card's hardware (MAC) address?
items.explanation: The address file holds the interface's MAC address. operstate reports up/down, and the other paths don't carry a MAC address.

lessons.title: Writable Settings: /proc/sys, sysctl & Tools
lessons.content:
Until now we've only *read* virtual files. A special corner of `/proc` lets you *write* them — and writing changes a live kernel setting.

**Hook.** Some of these virtual files aren't just gauges; they're switches. Flip one, and the system's behavior changes immediately.

**Build.** The folder `/proc/sys/` holds *tunable* kernel settings. Many of its files are writable, and writing a value changes a setting on the spot. A classic example is turning the machine into a router (passing network traffic between interfaces):

```bash
cat /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward     # shows 0 (off)
echo 1 > /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward  # now 1 (on), live
```

Typing those long paths is awkward, so there's a friendlier command: `sysctl`. It uses dotted names that map directly to the paths (dots become slashes): `net.ipv4.ip_forward` is the same setting as `/proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward`. Another common knob is `vm.swappiness`, which nudges how eagerly the system uses swap.

```bash
sysctl net.ipv4.ip_forward            # read it
sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_forward=1       # set it (same as the echo above)
```

One catch: these changes are live but **lost on reboot**. To make a setting permanent, put the line in a file under `/etc/sysctl.d/` (an ordinary on-disk config folder), e.g. `net.ipv4.ip_forward = 1` in `/etc/sysctl.d/99-router.conf`. It's reapplied at every boot.

Finally, the familiar monitoring tools — `free`, `vmstat`, `top` — are all just `/proc` readers underneath. They sample the same files you now know and present the numbers nicely.

**Recap.** `/proc/sys` (via the `sysctl` command) holds writable kernel settings; writing changes them live, and `/etc/sysctl.d/` makes them survive a reboot. Everyday tools like `free` and `vmstat` simply read `/proc`.

items.prompt: What is special about files under /proc/sys/ compared to most of /proc?
choices: They are stored on disk instead of being virtual
Many are writable, and writing one changes a live kernel setting
They can only be read by the root user
They contain the system's log files
items.explanation: /proc/sys holds tunable settings: many files are writable, and a write immediately changes kernel behavior. Most other /proc files are read-only views.

items.prompt: How does the sysctl name net.ipv4.ip_forward map to a file path?
items.explanation: sysctl dotted names map directly under /proc/sys with dots turned into slashes. So net.ipv4.ip_forward is /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward.

items.prompt: You ran `echo 1 > /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward` and forwarding turned on. After a reboot it's off again. Why?
choices: Writes to /proc/sys are live only and reset at boot; you must persist them in config
ip_forward can never be turned on
items.explanation: Runtime writes to /proc/sys don't survive a reboot. To keep the setting, you add it to persistent config; the kernel reapplies it at boot.

items.prompt: How do you make a sysctl setting survive reboots on a typical modern system?
choices: Add the line to a .conf file under /etc/sysctl.d/
items.explanation: A line such as net.ipv4.ip_forward = 1 in a file under /etc/sysctl.d/ is reapplied at every boot. Writing to /proc/sys alone is temporary.

items.prompt: What does the `sysctl` command give you over editing /proc/sys files directly?
choices: It is the only way to read settings
A friendlier dotted-name interface to the same settings, e.g. sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_forward=1
It changes settings permanently by itself
items.explanation: sysctl is a convenient front-end using dotted names that map to /proc/sys paths. By itself sysctl -w is still a runtime change, not a permanent one.

items.prompt: Turning on ip_forward does what?
choices: Lets the machine pass network traffic between interfaces, acting like a router
items.explanation: IP forwarding lets the system route packets between its network interfaces, so it can act as a router. It has nothing to do with CPU speed or disk space.

items.prompt: Tools like free and vmstat report memory and system activity. What are they doing underneath?
choices: Reading the same /proc files (like /proc/meminfo) and presenting the numbers
items.explanation: free, vmstat, and top are /proc readers: they sample the same files you can read by hand and format the results. They don't measure hardware directly or write settings.

items.prompt: Which two commands have the SAME effect of turning IP forwarding on right now?
items.explanation: Writing 1 to the file with echo and using sysctl -w on the matching dotted name do the same live change. The other pairs only read, list, reboot, or are invalid.

items.prompt: What does the vm.swappiness setting roughly control?
choices: How eagerly the system uses swap
The number of open files allowed
items.explanation: vm.swappiness is a tunable that nudges how readily the kernel moves memory to swap. It is set the same way as other sysctl values, and persisted via /etc/sysctl.d/.
